findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a well-circumscribed, round, oval, or lobulated mass located within the parotid gland. On MRI, it classically demonstrates T1 iso- to hypointensity and marked T2 hyperintensity.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include delayed, gradual contrast enhancement on dynamic post-gadolinium sequences. The mass is typically confined within the gland parenchyma without invading adjacent fat planes or musculature.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of irregular, indistinct margins fading into the surrounding gland, which would suggest a malignant infiltrative process. Furthermore, there is no evidence of invasion into adjacent structures such as the masticator space, and no pathological cervical lymphadenopathy.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a well-defined, discrete, unilateral parotid mass that is characteristically very bright on T2-weighted imaging.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Pleomorphic adenoma, given the well-circumscribed nature of the mass, its characteristic T2 hyperintensity, and delayed enhancement. This is the most common benign parotid neoplasm, frequently presenting as an asymptomatic palpable mass.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Warthin tumor, however these typically present as mixed cystic and solid lesions, often occur in middle-aged men, and have a propensity to be bilateral. Additionally, Warthin tumors usually do not exhibit the same degree of striking T2 hyperintensity as pleomorphic adenomas.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Parotid carcinoma, given that malignant neoplasms such as mucoepidermoid or adenoid cystic carcinoma can occasionally present as a seemingly circumscribed mass, and missing this could delay necessary radical resection and radiotherapy. However, carcinomas typically exhibit indistinct margins, lower T2 signal, and may show perineural spread or local invasion.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately localize the mass in relation to the facial nerve plane, which is extrapolated as a line connecting the stylomastoid foramen to the retromandibular vein, to guide surgical planning.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to evaluate the growth rate, as a sudden rapid increase in size might herald malignant transformation into a carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the neck with contrast if only a CT or ultrasound has been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating superficial versus deep lobe involvement, demonstrating the characteristic T2 hyperintensity, and evaluating for perineural spread.
management.mriSequences: I would specify high-resolution T2-weighted sequences to confirm the characteristic hyperintensity of a pleomorphic adenoma, alongside T1 pre- and post-contrast dynamic sequences to demonstrate its typical delayed enhancement pattern.
management.intervention: If tissue diagnosis is needed to definitively exclude a low-grade malignancy prior to surgery, I would specify an ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration (FNA) or core biopsy of the lesion.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Head and Neck MDT. My report would clearly document whether the mass is in the superficial or deep lobe relative to the retromandibular vein, its exact dimensions, and the absence of suspicious cervical lymph nodes.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a well-circumscribed, round, oval, or lobulated mass located within the parotid gland. On MRI, it classically demonstrates T1 iso- to hypointensity and marked T2 hyperintensity. Supporting features include delayed, gradual contrast enhancement on dynamic post-gadolinium sequences. The mass is typically confined within the gland parenchyma without invading adjacent fat planes or musculature. Importantly, there is no evidence of irregular, indistinct margins fading into the surrounding gland, which would suggest a malignant infiltrative process. Furthermore, there is no evidence of invasion into adjacent structures such as the masticator space, and no pathological cervical lymphadenopathy. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a well-defined, discrete, unilateral parotid mass that is characteristically very bright on T2-weighted imaging.

The appearances are most in keeping with Pleomorphic adenoma, given the well-circumscribed nature of the mass, its characteristic T2 hyperintensity, and delayed enhancement. This is the most common benign parotid neoplasm, frequently presenting as an asymptomatic palpable mass. The principle differential would be Warthin tumor, however these typically present as mixed cystic and solid lesions, often occur in middle-aged men, and have a propensity to be bilateral. Additionally, Warthin tumors usually do not exhibit the same degree of striking T2 hyperintensity as pleomorphic adenomas. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Parotid carcinoma, given that malignant neoplasms such as mucoepidermoid or adenoid cystic carcinoma can occasionally present as a seemingly circumscribed mass, and missing this could delay necessary radical resection and radiotherapy. However, carcinomas typically exhibit indistinct margins, lower T2 signal, and may show perineural spread or local invasion.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately localize the mass in relation to the facial nerve plane, which is extrapolated as a line connecting the stylomastoid foramen to the retromandibular vein, to guide surgical planning. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to evaluate the growth rate, as a sudden rapid increase in size might herald malignant transformation into a carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the neck with contrast if only a CT or ultrasound has been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating superficial versus deep lobe involvement, demonstrating the characteristic T2 hyperintensity, and evaluating for perineural spread. I would specify high-resolution T2-weighted sequences to confirm the characteristic hyperintensity of a pleomorphic adenoma, alongside T1 pre- and post-contrast dynamic sequences to demonstrate its typical delayed enhancement pattern. If tissue diagnosis is needed to definitively exclude a low-grade malignancy prior to surgery, I would specify an ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration (FNA) or core biopsy of the lesion. This should go to the Head and Neck MDT. My report would clearly document whether the mass is in the superficial or deep lobe relative to the retromandibular vein, its exact dimensions, and the absence of suspicious cervical lymph nodes.
